author: Dr. Priya Shah
content:
EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT NOTE — 11/4/2026, 9:30 PM
Patient: Sandoval, Maria Isabel (MRN: MRN-90215734)
DOB: 8/12/2003 (Age 23)
Triage time: 8:55 PM | Triage acuity: ESI 3
Interpreter: Spanish phone interpreter (Language Line) used throughout.

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
'Mi cuerpo no puede mas' — 'My body cannot do any more.' Worsening fatigue, joint pain, intermittent rash, and feeling weak.

HPI:
Ms. Sandoval is a 23-year-old Spanish-speaking woman with known undifferentiated connective tissue disease / probable early SLE, Hashimoto's thyroiditis on levothyroxine, recurrent medication interruptions due to financial barriers, who presents to the ED tonight with worsening of her chronic symptoms after the most recent disruption in her hydroxychloroquine supply.

Recent course (per available Hopkins/Esperanza records and patient/mother report):
- 9/15/2026 (Dr. Williams, PCP): Restarted HCQ via low-cost cash-pay 30-day bridge; weight 46.4 kg; new cytopenias (Hgb 11.1, WBC 3.85); albumin 3.4; ESR 38, CRP 1.8.
- Late September 2026: Hopkins rheum (Dr. Tanaka) saw her urgently — visit fee covered through new financial-aid grant. Confirmed disease worsening, continued HCQ, considered increasing dose, deferred starting steroid-sparing immunosuppressant pending lab confirmation of evolving phenotype, recommended close monitoring.
- October 2026: 30-day cash-pay HCQ supply exhausted ~10/15/2026. Mother and patient attempted again to bridge with PAP — submitted but processing pending. Patient has been off HCQ for approximately 3 weeks.
- Last week (10/28/2026): She felt 'a bad day' and stayed home from work. She has not worked in approximately 8 days. She tried to come to Esperanza walk-in but the wait was too long for her to manage with her fatigue level and she went home.

Today's symptoms (specifically):
- Severe fatigue: She estimates she has slept 14-18 hours/day for the past 3 days. Difficulty getting out of bed.
- Joint pain: Diffuse, bilateral. Morning stiffness now lasts most of the day. Knees, wrists, hands, ankles all painful.
- Rash: Persistent over chest and upper back; today she also notes some pinkish patches on her thighs. Not itchy or painful.
- Weight: Mother says she has visibly lost more weight ('she is bones'). Patient does not know specific weight.
- Appetite: Very poor. Mother estimates she has eaten 'maybe one tortilla and some beans' today.
- Headache: Mild, intermittent over the past week.
- New: Mild dyspnea on exertion when climbing the stairs to her apartment (she lives on a 3rd floor walkup). Not at rest. No chest pain. No cough.
- Mood: Persistently low. PHQ-9 administered in triage = 14 (moderately severe). Denies SI/HI.
- Specifically denied tonight: high fever, syncope, focal weakness, vision changes, dark or bloody urine, foamy urine, oral ulcers, dysphagia for food (she ate the tortilla and beans without choking), severe abdominal pain.

Medications: Levothyroxine 25 mcg PO daily (continued). HCQ — not taking (out since ~10/15/2026). Tylenol 1g PRN, has been taking for joint pain — estimates ~3g/day.

Allergies: NKDA.
Family Hx, Social Hx: As prior. She is brought in by her mother and aunt; no recent travel; non-sexual; uses public transit.

VITALS ON ARRIVAL (8:55 PM):
BP 96/58, HR 108, RR 18, T 98.4 F, SpO2 96% RA. Weight 43.8 kg (down from 46.4 on 9/15/2026 — additional 2.6 kg loss in 7 weeks; total loss now 7.0 kg from 1/12/2026 baseline of 50.8 kg, or ~13.8%). Height 150 cm. BMI 19.5 (lower limit of normal). Pain 7/10.

PHYSICAL EXAM:
General: Thin young woman who appears exhausted and chronically ill. Sitting up with mother holding her arm. Engaged through interpreter. No acute distress at rest.
HEENT: Pale conjunctivae. Oropharynx clear, NO ulcers identified (carefully inspected hard and soft palate, tongue, buccal mucosa). Lips slightly dry. No cervical lymphadenopathy. Thyroid not enlarged.
Skin: Pink, faintly erythematous, blanching macular rash distributed over V-area of chest, upper back, and outer thighs. Not raised. No discoid lesions. No vasculitic changes. Diffuse scalp hair thinning marked; visible scalp at crown and frontal hairline. Nail beds pale; no nail-fold capillary changes to gross inspection. No livedo. Hands: dry, mildly scaly skin over knuckles bilaterally (subtle).
CV: Tachycardic, regular rhythm. No murmurs. JVP not elevated. Capillary refill 2 seconds.
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles, wheeze, or rub. RR 18.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No organomegaly. Normoactive bowel sounds.
Extremities: No edema. Mild bilateral synovitis of MCP, PIP, wrists. Left knee with small effusion, ROM limited by pain. Ankles tender. Grip strength reduced bilaterally (3/5 estimated). Proximal muscle strength: 4/5 hip flexion, 4/5 shoulder abduction; some give-way, possibly due to pain rather than weakness; uncertain.
Neuro: Alert, oriented x3. Cranial nerves intact. DTRs 2+ symmetrically. Sensation intact. Gait slow but steady, no ataxia.

ED LABS (drawn 9:15 PM):
- CBC: WBC 3.10 (low), Hgb 10.2 (low; down from 11.1), MCV 86, Plt 184 (low-normal); ANC 1.6 (mildly low); lymphocytes 0.9 (low — lymphopenia)
- CMP: Na 135, K 3.5, Cl 99, CO2 22, BUN 6, Cr 0.5, Glu 78, Ca 8.4 (low-normal), Albumin 3.0 (low; down from 3.4), Total protein 6.1 (low), AST 32, ALT 26, ALP 76, Total bilirubin 0.5
- Mg 1.8 (low-normal), Phos 3.2, Lactate 1.4
- TSH 5.04 (mildly elevated; medication adherence vs progression)
- ESR 52 (elevated)
- CRP 2.3 (elevated)
- UA: 1+ protein, no blood, no leukocytes; protein:creatinine ratio 0.35 (mildly elevated, ref <0.2)
- Anti-dsDNA: low-positive at 24 IU/mL (ref <10); first positive in her history
- C3: 76 mg/dL (LOW; ref 90-180)
- C4: 8 mg/dL (LOW; ref 10-40)
- Pregnancy test: negative
- Troponin: <0.01 (not concerning)
- BNP: 42 (not elevated)
- Blood cultures: drawn, pending

IMAGING:
- Chest X-ray (PA and lateral): Lungs clear. No effusion, no infiltrate. Heart size normal. No widened mediastinum. No previous comparison available in ED system. Read by ED radiologist Dr. Patel.
- EKG: NSR at 102, no acute ST changes.

ED COURSE:
Given the clinical picture — significant weight loss, new positive dsDNA, low complements, mild proteinuria, lymphopenia, anemia — the working diagnosis is now SHIFTING TOWARD SLE flare (now meeting more 2019 EULAR/ACR criteria: ANA entry; immunologic criteria with low complements and dsDNA; hematologic with leukopenia/lymphopenia; mucocutaneous with rash; constitutional weight loss; mild renal with proteinuria). Patient discussed with Hopkins rheumatology on call (Dr. Hassan).

Hopkins rheumatology recommendations (verbal, 11:15 PM):
- Admit for inpatient evaluation, initiation of higher-intensity SLE management, and characterization of suspected renal involvement and constitutional decline.
- Recommend addition of prednisone 0.5 mg/kg/day (initial), with consideration of pulse steroids only after confirming infectious workup is clean.
- Strongly consider obtaining a renal biopsy if proteinuria progresses.
- Continue levothyroxine, restart hydroxychloroquine if confirmed available, hold NSAIDs.
- Address adherence/access issues with social work.

The patient/mother were informed of the recommendation for admission. The patient and mother declined inpatient admission tonight, citing financial concerns and ability to be managed at home. After extensive discussion (in Spanish, ~45 minutes) regarding the seriousness of the findings and the significant risk of progression, particularly with respect to kidney involvement, the patient still preferred to go home with a strong plan and close follow-up. She agreed to come back IMMEDIATELY for any worsening or new symptoms. I documented the AMA-against-recommendation discussion and provided extensive return precautions in Spanish (written).

DISCHARGE PLAN:
1. AGAINST RECOMMENDATION: Patient declined admission. Strong recommendation for admission documented. Risks of progression discussed in detail.
2. Prescribed: Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg PO BID — 30-day ED-pharmacy supply provided FREE through hospital charity care assist (one-time bridge).
3. Prescribed: Prednisone 30 mg PO daily (initial dose) x 7 days, then re-evaluate in clinic. Patient instructed to take with food.
4. Provided: Calcium carbonate 500 mg BID + vitamin D 1000 IU daily; PPI omeprazole 20 mg daily for GI prophylaxis.
5. Continue levothyroxine.
6. Hold acetaminophen given LFTs are okay but to minimize. Hold ibuprofen given proteinuria.
7. Strict precautions: ED return for ANY new fever, chest pain, dyspnea worse than tonight, severe headache, focal weakness, vision change, dark or foamy urine, leg/facial swelling, oral ulcers, abdominal pain, or significant worsening of joint pain or rash.
8. Follow-up appointments arranged tonight by ED case manager:
   - Hopkins rheumatology (Dr. Tanaka): 11/9/2026 at 9 AM (financial-aid grant obtained tonight by case manager for this visit and one additional).
   - Esperanza Health PCP (Dr. Williams): 11/10/2026 at 1 PM.
   - Social work (Ms. Mercado): will call patient 11/5/2026.
9. Outpatient labs to be repeated 11/7/2026: CBC, CMP, ESR, CRP, UPC, complements, dsDNA — order placed.
10. Renal biopsy: deferred for outpatient discussion if proteinuria persists.

Total ED time: 7 hours. Total interpreter time: 85 minutes.

Discharge condition: Stable but high-risk. Patient ambulatory with mother. Patient verbalized understanding of return precautions in Spanish.

PATIENT INSTRUCTIONS:
'Take the new medications exactly as prescribed. Return immediately if you develop any of the warning signs we discussed. Keep your appointments at Hopkins and at Esperanza. If you cannot keep them, call us. This is a serious time — you may need to be in the hospital soon.'
attestation:
Electronically signed by: Dr. Priya Shah, MD
Department of Emergency Medicine, Johns Hopkins Hospital
Date: 11/4/2026
